straight_beat.name: Bobby Lee Chamberlain
straight_beat.column:
The Royals didn’t just beat the White Sox yesterday—they did it with the kind of poise that makes you think Ned Yost might still be scribbling notes on a legal pad in his office, muttering about “the Midwest box” and how it’s supposed to be a place where you don’t let the game slip away. Two runs, a bullpen that held the White Sox to zero, and a lineup that finally figured out how to scratch out some offense. It wasn’t pretty, but it was necessary.  

The pitching staff, again, was the backbone of this win. Daniel Lynch, who’s been a bit of a wildcard this season, settled in after the first inning and kept the White Sox hitters off balance. You could see it in the way the hitters swung at pitches outside the strike zone, like they were trying to prove a point to someone. Lynch didn’t have his best stuff, but he had enough to get the job done, and that’s what

optimist.name: Dottie Verlander (no relation)
optimist.column:
The Royals won yesterday. That’s the fact. A 2-0 shutout over the White Sox, a team that’s been a thorn in the side of this division for years. The scoreboard says 6-8, 2.0 games back in the AL Central, but let’s not let the numbers do the talking. This team is still in the game, and that’s what matters.  

Bobby Witt Jr. is the heartbeat of this roster. You’ve heard me say it before, but I’ll say it again: Witt’s not just a prospect. He’s a prospect with a pedigree. Remember when he was in Double-A, and I told everyone he’d one day walk into a ballpark like George Brett did in the ‘70s? Well, here he is, already making hitters look like they’re swinging at shadows. Yesterday, he didn’t just hit a home run—he hit a home run. A two-run shot that cleared the fence, the kind of swing that makes you think, *this is how it’s done*.  

And let’s not forget the pitching staff. They’ve been the unsung heroes, holding opponents to two runs or fewer in four of their last five games. Luis Gil, that kid from the Dominican Republic, is throwing like a veteran. He’s got that fire, that intensity you see in guys who’ve been waiting for their shot. And yes, I know he’s still learning, but learning is what you do when you’re 24 and trying to make a name for yourself.  

The front office has been busy, too. They’re not just sitting around waiting for the season to turn. They’ve been moving pieces, trading for talent, drafting smart, and building a farm system that’s finally starting to show some fruit. You can’t ignore the fact that this team is still in the race. They’re not just surviving—they’re adapting.  

Now, I know what some of you are thinking: *Six wins in 14 games? That’s not enough.* But here’s the thing: baseball is

pessimist.name: Cletus Marburger
pessimist.column:
The Royals lost again yesterday, 2-0 to the White Sox. Another loss. Another step closer to the 1985 magic number. Wait, no—1985 was the year they won the World Series. This is 2023. The Royals are 6-8, 2.0 games behind in the AL Central. It’s not just the record that’s troubling. It’s the way the team keeps looking like a collection of parts that don’t fit together. You get one or two good players, they get traded. You get a ring, they fire-sale the roster. David Glass lives in every Royals decision, even now.  

The front office has been busy making moves, but the results? Not much. They traded away prospects for middling free agents, hoping to fill gaps that never really existed. Maybe they thought the fans would forget the last 38 years of futility. But we
